title: Types of Innovation: the 4Ps & Disruption
simple_html:
Not all innovation is the same. This lesson gives you two simple maps for sorting innovations: the 4Ps (what you change) and the idea of disruption (how it shakes up a market).

The 4Ps of innovation

You can innovate along four dimensions:

  • Product — the goods or services you offer (e.g., a new smartphone).
  • Process — how you make or deliver them (e.g., Toyota's lean production).
  • Position — who you target and how it's framed (e.g., Lucozade going from "sick-room drink" to "sports drink").
  • Paradigm — the whole mental model or business model (e.g., budget airlines rethinking what flying is).

How big a change?

Innovations also vary by degree: incremental (small improvements to what exists) versus radical (brand-new, game-changing). Most innovation is incremental, but radical innovation reshapes industries.

Disruptive innovation

Clayton Christensen showed that simple, cheaper, "good-enough" products often start at the bottom of a market (or in a brand-new market), get better over time, and eventually displace established leaders. Incumbents ignore them because they look low-quality and low-profit — until it's too late. Netflix vs. Blockbuster is the classic example.

Key takeaways

  • Innovation can target Product, Process, Position, or Paradigm.
  • Changes range from incremental to radical.
  • Disruption isn't just "better" — it's a different, simpler trajectory that overtakes incumbents from below or from new markets.
  • Watching only your existing best customers can blind you to disruptive threats.
intermediate_html:
This lesson equips you to classify innovations and to reason about which ones threaten or favour your firm. Two frameworks dominate: Tidd and Bessant's 4Ps (the what of innovation space) and Christensen's disruptive innovation (the how of competitive dynamics). We also place incremental vs. radical change on the degree-of-novelty axis.

The 4Ps of innovation

The 4Ps framework maps the territory an organisation can innovate in:

  • Product innovation — changes in the things (products/services) a firm offers. Example: the electric vehicle versus the combustion car.
  • Process innovation — changes in how products/services are created and delivered. Example: Ford's moving assembly line; Amazon's fulfilment robotics.
  • Position innovation — changes in the context/market in which products are introduced or how they are perceived. Example: rebranding Lucozade from a convalescence drink to an energy/sports drink.
  • Paradigm innovation — changes in the underlying mental models that frame what the organisation does. Example: low-cost carriers (Ryanair, Southwest) reframing air travel as a no-frills commodity.

Each P also varies in degree from incremental ("doing what we do better") to radical ("doing something completely different"). Tidd and Bessant visualise this as innovation space — a firm should consciously choose where in that space to play.

Incremental vs. radical

Most innovation is incremental: cumulative small improvements that sustain existing trajectories and are usually led by incumbents. Radical innovation introduces fundamentally new technologies or concepts, carries higher uncertainty and reward, and often originates with new entrants. A related distinction (Henderson & Clark) is architectural innovation — reconfiguring how existing components link together — which can quietly destroy incumbent competence even without new components.

Disruptive innovation

Clayton Christensen's The Innovator's Dilemma explains why well-managed incumbents fail. Sustaining innovations improve products along dimensions mainstream customers already value; incumbents almost always win these. Disruptive innovations start as simpler, cheaper, lower-performing offerings that appeal to overserved or non-consumers, then improve until they satisfy the mainstream — at which point they capture the market. Two flavours: low-end disruption (cheaper, good-enough offerings attacking overserved customers, e.g., Korean steel mini-mills) and new-market disruption (serving people who previously consumed nothing, e.g., the transistor radio, personal computers).

Worked example: Netflix vs. Blockbuster

Netflix's DVD-by-mail (and later streaming) was initially inferior to Blockbuster on the metric Blockbuster valued — immediate, in-store availability of new releases. It served convenience-seeking, price-sensitive customers Blockbuster overlooked. As streaming improved, it overtook the incumbent. Blockbuster, focused on its most profitable store and late-fee model, dismissed the threat — the classic innovator's dilemma: rational attention to current best customers blinds incumbents to disruption.

How to apply it

  • Audit your innovation across all 4Ps. Firms over-invest in product and neglect process, position, and paradigm — often where the biggest gains lie.
  • Classify a competitor's move: Is it sustaining (you can out-compete) or disruptive (it follows a different trajectory you may need to address with a separate unit)?
  • Watch non-consumption and overserved segments — disruption's launchpads — not just your top accounts.
  • Match degree to capability: radical/architectural moves may require ring-fenced teams, since they can render existing competences obsolete.

These categories feed directly into the Technology S-curve (where disruptions ride a new curve) and connect to Lesson 6's diffusion and Lesson 7's ambidexterity for managing both sustaining and disruptive work.
full_html:
Classifying innovation is not academic tidiness — it determines strategy. The type of innovation dictates which capabilities, organisational structures, and competitive responses are appropriate. This lesson integrates the principal taxonomies: the 4Ps (Tidd & Bessant), the incremental–radical and competence dimensions (Henderson & Clark), and the disruption framework (Christensen), and situates them against the technology S-curve.

The 4Ps: mapping innovation space

Tidd and Bessant (2018) argue innovation can target four domains, the 4Ps: Product (changes in offerings), Process (changes in how offerings are created/delivered), Position (changes in the context or perception in which they are introduced), and Paradigm (changes in the underlying mental/business models). Each can be pursued incrementally or radically, generating an "innovation space." The framework's value is diagnostic: most firms reflexively equate innovation with new products and systematically under-exploit process, position, and paradigm. Dell's direct-to-customer paradigm, Zara's fast-fashion process, and Nintendo Wii's positional shift toward casual gamers each created more value than incremental product tweaks would have.

Degree and locus of novelty

Along the novelty axis, incremental innovation refines existing trajectories (low uncertainty, typically incumbent-led, and the dominant share of all innovation), while radical innovation introduces fundamentally new concepts (high uncertainty, often entrant-led). Abernathy and Clark (1985) refined this with the notion that innovation can be competence-enhancing or competence-destroying for incumbents. Henderson and Clark (1990) added a crucial second dimension by distinguishing changes in components from changes in architecture (how components are linked). Their 2x2 yields four types: incremental, modular, architectural, and radical. The subtle insight is that architectural innovation — reconfiguring linkages while keeping components — can devastate incumbents precisely because it is hard to see: established firms have organisational structures and information filters built around the old architecture (Xerox vs. small copiers is their canonical case).

Christensen's disruptive innovation

Clayton Christensen's The Innovator's Dilemma (Christensen, 1997) is the field's most influential — and most misused — theory. Its logic: technological progress often outpaces customers' ability to absorb it, creating a performance overshoot. Sustaining innovations push performance along dimensions mainstream customers value; incumbents, organised to satisfy and listen to their best customers, almost always win these contests. Disruptive innovations enter at the bottom — simpler, cheaper, more convenient, initially worse on traditional metrics — appealing either to overserved low-end customers (low-end disruption) or to non-consumers for whom the alternative was nothing (new-market disruption). Because these footholds are small and low-margin, rational incumbents cede them; but as the disruptor improves along its own trajectory it climbs into the mainstream and displaces the incumbent. Christensen and Raynor (2003) formalised the low-end vs. new-market distinction, and Christensen, Raynor and McDonald (2015) later policed the term's misuse, stressing that Uber, for instance, is not disruptive in the strict sense because it did not originate in low-end or non-consumption.

The technology S-curve

Foster's (1986) S-curve links these ideas: a technology's performance rises slowly, then steeply, then plateaus as it matures. Disruptions typically ride a new S-curve that starts below the incumbent curve but has a steeper or higher ceiling; incumbents over-invest in the flattening top of the old curve while the new curve overtakes them. Recognising S-curve inflection points is central to timing innovation investment and avoiding the "sailing-ship effect" of squeezing a dying technology.

Worked example: steel mini-mills (low-end) and PCs (new-market)

Nucor's mini-mills began producing low-quality rebar — the least profitable segment that integrated mills happily abandoned. From that beachhead Nucor moved up-market into bars, structural steel, and finally sheet steel, displacing integrated producers tier by tier: textbook low-end disruption. The personal computer, by contrast, did not steal minicomputer customers; it created a vast new market of individual users who had never owned a computer (new-market disruption), eventually rendering minicomputers obsolete. Mapping a case to the correct disruption type determines the right response — co-opt the low end, or stand up an autonomous new-market venture.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • "Disruptive" = any breakthrough. The most common error. Disruption is a specific trajectory from low-end/new-market footholds, not a synonym for radical or successful.
  • Equating innovation with product/technology. The 4Ps explicitly correct this; process and paradigm innovation are frequently more defensible.
  • Listening only to best customers. The very practice that makes incumbents excellent at sustaining innovation blinds them to disruption — the innovator's dilemma.
  • Ignoring architecture. Firms benchmark components and miss competence-destroying architectural shifts (Henderson & Clark, 1990).

How to apply the typologies

  • Run a 4Ps audit to find under-exploited innovation space; deliberately set a portfolio across product, process, position, paradigm.
  • Classify each threat/opportunity on two axes — degree (incremental↔radical) and trajectory (sustaining↔disruptive) — to choose response and structure.
  • For disruptive threats, follow Christensen's prescription: house the response in an autonomous unit with its own cost structure and metrics (linking to ambidexterity, Lesson 7).
  • Track S-curves to time exit from maturing technologies and entry into new ones.

Connections to the syllabus

This taxonomy underpins the rest of the course. The innovation process (Lesson 3) must be calibrated to type — radical work needs more exploration. Diffusion and crossing the chasm (Lesson 6) explain how disruptive and radical innovations spread through adopter segments. Building the innovative organisation (Lesson 7) addresses the structural ambidexterity disruption demands. And business-model thinking (Lesson 5) often is paradigm innovation in the 4Ps sense.
